meta.title: Nelson Mandela: Freedom for All
meta.author: Cynthia Kennedy Henzel
meta.focusQuestion: How did Nelson Mandela's personality, accomplishments, and influence lead to the end of apartheid and freedom for all South Africans?

sections.title: Robben Island
paragraphs: Nelson Mandela sat on the hard ground of Robben Island before a pile of rocks. A guard gave him a hammer and ordered him to break the rocks into gravel. Other prisoners sat in rows near him, but they were not allowed to speak to each other.

Mandela, forty-four years old, had been sentenced to life in prison on Robben Island, a small island off the coast of South Africa. Prisoners worked all day breaking rocks. They ate bad food, slept in tiny cells, and were treated poorly by the guards. With no newspapers or radios, the prisoners were cut off from the rest of the world.

Mandela missed his children and his wife. Every six months, he was allowed one short letter, which was opened and censored by the prison. Every six months, he was allowed one visitor, who had to speak to him through a glass window. Sitting in the dirty prison yard, Mandela longed for the open fields and freedom of his childhood. How had he ended up here?

sections.title: A Free-Roaming Life
paragraphs: Nelson Mandela was born in 1918 in a country that was very different from the South Africa of today. The Dutch and British had colonized the land centuries before. Millions of black South Africans were forced under their control. The country was rich with diamond and gold mines, but the riches were not for everyone. A small minority of white people had all the good jobs and owned ninety percent of the land.

Nelson's father, Chief Henry, was from a royal family of the Thembu (TEM-boo) tribe and had helped advise the Thembu rulers. However, a local British official became angry at him and took his title and money when Nelson was a baby. Nelson and his mother lived in a village near his mother's family. His father was often away at the homes of his three other wives.

At birth, Nelson Mandela was named Rolihlahla, which means "one who shakes the tree" in the language of the Thembu. Tree shaker is a term that means "someone who causes trouble or sudden change," like when someone shakes an apple tree, causing all the fruit to tumble down at once. When Rolihlahla began school at age seven, his teacher gave him a British name. He became Nelson Mandela.

Nelson was happy playing with other boys, herding sheep and cattle, and going to school. Then, when he was nine, his father died, and Nelson's free-roaming life ended. One morning, his mother woke him early and told him to pack everything he owned in a bag. As they began a long walk along dusty roads, Nelson looked back at the family's round mud hut. He feared he would never see his home again.

sections.title: Learning to Listen
paragraphs: Late in the evening, they came to the home of Chief Jongintaba, leader of the Thembu people. Nelson had never seen anything like the large herds of cattle and grand houses of the "Great Place." Chief Henry had been a good friend to Chief Jongintaba, and the important man wanted to raise Nelson. So Nelson's mother left her son in his care.

Nelson was lucky to live in the Great Place. He became friends and went to school with the chief's son, Justice. They went to meetings and listened as Chief Jongintaba settled arguments among his people. Nelson saw how the chief listened to both sides before making a decision. Nelson heard stories about those who came before him and learned about the ways of his people. He came to believe that he could be a leader.

sections.title: The Big City
paragraphs: Nelson did well in school, so Chief Jongintaba sent him to college in Fort Hare. Here, Nelson got into his first bit of trouble. He quit the student council in order to show support for students who were protesting the poor food at the college. The head of the college gave him a choice: Nelson could rejoin the student council to show support for the college or be kicked out. Nelson refused to give in and was sent home.

Chief Jongintaba was angry and decided that it was time for both Justice and Nelson to get married. The young men were shocked! They did not want to marry the girls whom the chief had chosen for them. Instead, they sold one of the chief's prize oxen to get money and ran away to Johannesburg, the largest city in South Africa.

Justice got a job as a mine clerk, and Nelson became a security guard. Chief Jongintaba soon found them and demanded that they return home. Justice returned, but Nelson decided to stay alone in the big city.

Nelson made friends, moved into a tiny room, and finished his college degree by mail. One of his new friends got him a job at a law office where he could work as a clerk while he studied for a law degree. The friend also brought Nelson into the African National Congress (ANC), a group that fought for equal rights for blacks.
Soon Nelson marched in his first protest, to stop bus fare increases for poor workers. People boycotted the buses, refusing to ride them. After only nine days, the fares were reduced.

Nelson was excited by their success, and in 1944 he helped found the ANC Youth League (ANCYL) as part of the ANC. The ANCYL wanted to plan bigger protests and boycotts. Within a few years, the group would have more cause to protest than ever before.

sections.title: From Bad to Worse
paragraphs: Only white people could vote in South Africa, and in 1948 they elected D. F. Malan of the Afrikaner National Party as president. That party believed in apartheid (uh-PAR-tide), a policy that forces different races to stay segregated from each other, with different rights.
Malan supported a policy of apartheid—"apart-ness" in Afrikaans, the language of the descendants of the first Dutch settlers.

All black men had to carry a pass to travel, and blacks could not be trained for good jobs. Every person was labeled by race: black, white, Coloured (mixed race), or Indian. People of different races could not marry, and each race had to live in its own area. People were often labeled by their looks, so family members might end up in different groups if some had lighter skin than others. Families with members in different groups could not live together. They could not even meet in parks or other public places because those areas were segregated, too.
Mandela was now married with children to care for and worked long hours at the law office. Still, as conditions got worse, he fought harder for justice and became the president of the ANCYL in 1951.

sections.title: A Rising Star
paragraphs: The next year, the African National Congress urged people not to follow the apartheid laws. Blacks burned their travel passes and sat down in Whites Only parts of buses. They drank from Whites Only water fountains and played in Whites Only parks. Mandela spoke before thousands of protesters and was arrested. He was forbidden by law to travel, give speeches, or hold public office.
Mandela continued to work for the ANC in secret. He also helped start the first black law office in South Africa, to help people who were suffering under apartheid.

In 1955, Mandela was arrested again and charged with treason, or trying to bring down the government. He was released from jail for a time, but the ban on speaking and travel continued. Unhappy with the risks he took and the dangers he faced, his first wife divorced him.

During the four-and-a-half-year trial, Mandela continued his work with the ANC in secret, but conditions got worse. At a protest in 1960, police fired at peaceful protesters and killed sixty-nine people. The ANC was banned.

Soon after, Mandela's trial ended when he was found not guilty of treason. Yet Mandela knew that unless he gave up the fight for equal rights, he would soon be arrested again. Since he refused to give up the fight, he went into hiding.

Mandela felt that all those years of peaceful protests had failed, and the ANC needed to try something else. In 1962, he escaped South Africa to ask leaders in other countries to help. Some gave him money for the fight, and others offered military training. He learned for the first time how to shoot a gun and make a bomb.

A year later, Mandela slipped back into South Africa and started a new part of the ANC called the Spear of the Nation (MK). The MK wanted to force the people in power to listen to them. They planned to do this by using sabotage, such as bombing buildings or power stations. Unfortunately, as a result of the bombings, people died.

Mandela made his plans at the MK's secret hideout. He traveled in disguise and became famous as the Black Pimpernel, a Robin Hood–type character who slipped past government traps aimed at catching him.

In 1962, Mandela was caught and sent to prison. Then the police found Mandela's secret hideout and his plans. He was charged with conspiracy to bring down the government, a crime for which he could be put to death. This time the verdict came quickly: guilty.
Mandela knew he would rather die than give up fighting for justice, so he prepared his final words in case he was to be hanged. The judge instead sentenced him to life in prison.

sections.title: Prison Years
paragraphs: Mandela's early years in prison were hard. Although the prisoners were not allowed to speak to one another, they found secret means of communicating, such as leaving notes under rocks or taped under toilet seats. Mandela demanded rights for the prisoners and that all races be treated with dignity. The guards came to respect him. Over time, the prisoners got better food, books so they could study, and more freedom to talk.

Mandela became a leader among the prisoners, who were men of different races. He used the listening skills he had learned as a child so that everyone felt he understood their side of the story. Many blacks thought that South Africa should be only for blacks because they were there first. Mandela had once believed this, too, but he now realized that all races should have a place in a truly free country.
Mandela's family and friends made sure the prisoners on Robben Island were not forgotten. They led protests and boycotts, and worked outside the country to make Mandela's name known everywhere.

Still, the government became more violent—protesters were murdered or simply disappeared. Even children were attacked when they protested laws that forced them to use the Afrikaans language in schools rather than their own language.

Other countries boycotted South Africa by not trading with them. By the 1980s, people around the world demanded an end to apartheid. The South African government wanted Mandela to cooperate, so they moved him to a better prison on the mainland. They promised to free him if he spoke up against violence and the MK, but he refused unless the government cast off apartheid.
In 1989, F. W. de Klerk was elected president. Within months, he let Mandela go and allowed the ANC to meet. Nelson Mandela, age seventy-one, walked free for the first time in twenty-seven years.
In 1993, Mandela shared the Nobel Peace Prize with de Klerk, but his work was not done. Now he worked to bring about free elections.

sections.title: Five-Hour Lines
paragraphs: The country's first free elections took place in 1994. Thousands of people stood in lines for up to five hours to vote for the first time. Nelson Mandela was elected president of South Africa.

Mandela did not want his country filled with hate and fear over things that had happened in the past, so he helped start the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC). A person could come before the TRC and confess to things he or she had done during apartheid and not be charged with a crime.

Some considered the TRC process unfair; however, it helped bring to light the truth about crimes that happened under apartheid. Those who had suffered under the old laws sometimes discovered what had happened to friends or relatives who had disappeared.

Mandela retired after his first term as president. He finally moved back to his childhood village, but he did not retire from helping his people. He met with world leaders to advance his country and peace around the world, and he started the Nelson Mandela Children's Fund to help children. After a long illness, he died in 2013.

Mandela's belief in talking with everyone, rather than just one side, helped to establish a country where all people are treated equally under the law. Today, Nelson Mandela remains a lasting symbol of freedom for all.

vocabulary.definition: formally declared a punishment given to someone by a court of law
vocabulary.sentence: Mandela, forty-four years old, had been sentenced to life in prison on Robben Island, a small island off the coast of South Africa.

options: given a punishment by a court

vocabulary.definition: removed words or other content viewed as threatening or offensive
vocabulary.sentence: Every six months, he was allowed one short letter, which was opened and censored by the prison.

quiz.prompt: If a letter is "censored," it has been _________.

vocabulary.definition: established an area or a country that was ruled by or belonged to another country
vocabulary.sentence: The Dutch and British had colonized the land centuries before.

options: given to another country as a gift

vocabulary.definition: a small group within a larger population; less than half
vocabulary.sentence: A small minority of white people had all the good jobs and owned ninety percent of the land.

options: a smaller group within a larger population
all the people in a country
the group with the most power

vocabulary.sentence: He quit the student council in order to show support for students who were protesting the poor food at the college.

vocabulary.definition: refused to buy or take part in something in order to make a point or force a change
vocabulary.sentence: People boycotted the buses, refusing to ride them.

options: refused to buy or use them

vocabulary.definition: an official policy of racial segregation in South Africa that discriminated against people who were not white
vocabulary.sentence: That party believed in apartheid, a policy that forces different races to stay segregated from each other, with different rights.

vocabulary.definition: kept apart based on group differences, such as race
vocabulary.sentence: They could not even meet in parks or other public places because those areas were segregated, too.

vocabulary.definition: the intentional destruction of or interference with something in order to make it not work
vocabulary.sentence: They planned to do this by using sabotage, such as bombing buildings or power stations.

options: intentional destruction to make something not work

vocabulary.definition: the act of two or more people secretly plotting to do something illegal or harmful
vocabulary.sentence: He was charged with conspiracy to bring down the government, a crime for which he could be put to death.

options: a secret plot to do something illegal

vocabulary.sentence: Mandela demanded rights for the prisoners and that all races be treated with dignity.

vocabulary.definition: the process of two or more people or groups settling a disagreement and becoming friendly again
vocabulary.sentence: Mandela did not want his country filled with hate and fear over things that had happened in the past, so he helped start the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC).

options: the process of settling disagreements and becoming friendly again

questions.question: What makes this book an informational biography?
questions.evidence: The book tells the true story of Nelson Mandela's life from birth in 1918 through his death in 2013, covering real events and real people.
choices: It is a factual book that gives information about real events.
It is a fictional story about a person.
It is written from the imagination of the writer.
All of the above
questions.explanation: A biography is a factual (nonfiction) book that gives information about real events in a real person's life. This book tells the true story of Nelson Mandela.

questions.question: The death of Nelson's father when he was nine caused him to _________.
questions.evidence: Then, when he was nine, his father died, and Nelson's free-roaming life ended. One morning, his mother woke him early and told him to pack everything he owned in a bag. As they began a long walk along dusty roads, Nelson looked back at the family's round mud hut.
choices: become angry and troubled
go to college immediately
move to another village with his mother
run away to the mountains
questions.explanation: When Nelson's father died, his mother took him on a long walk along dusty roads to the home of Chief Jongintaba in another village. His free-roaming life ended and he moved away from his childhood home.

questions.question: When people in Johannesburg boycotted the buses, they _________.
questions.evidence: People boycotted the buses, refusing to ride them.
choices: refused to ride them
stood in front of them so they couldn't drive
smashed the windows out
planted bombs on them
questions.explanation: The text directly states that people boycotted the buses by refusing to ride them. A boycott means refusing to buy or take part in something to force a change.

questions.question: How did Mandela finally get out of prison?
questions.evidence: In 1989, F. W. de Klerk was elected president. Within months, he let Mandela go and allowed the ANC to meet. Nelson Mandela, age seventy-one, walked free for the first time in twenty-seven years.
choices: The prison rioted, and Mandela escaped.
A new president was elected, and he released Mandela.
Mandela served his full term and got out of prison.
The government released all the prisoners.
questions.explanation: In 1989, F. W. de Klerk was elected president. Within months, he released Mandela and allowed the ANC to meet again. Mandela walked free at age 71 after 27 years in prison.

questions.question: After Mandela was released from prison, he _________.
questions.evidence: In 1993, Mandela shared the Nobel Peace Prize with de Klerk... The country's first free elections took place in 1994... Nelson Mandela was elected president of South Africa.
choices: won the Nobel Peace Prize
organized free elections
became president of South Africa
all of the above
questions.explanation: After his release, Mandela did ALL of these things: he shared the Nobel Peace Prize in 1993, worked for free elections, and was elected president of South Africa in 1994.

questions.question: What did Nelson Mandela do when he retired from the presidency of South Africa?
questions.evidence: Mandela retired after his first term as president. He finally moved back to his childhood village, but he did not retire from helping his people. He met with world leaders to advance his country and peace around the world, and he started the Nelson Mandela Children's Fund to help children.
choices: He worked with world leaders to advance peace around the world.
He went back to herding sheep and cattle.
He moved to America to continue the civil rights movement.
He traveled and enjoyed his retirement.
questions.explanation: After retiring, Mandela met with world leaders to advance his country and peace around the world, and he started the Nelson Mandela Children's Fund to help children.

questions.question: What is one way that Nelson Mandela was different from Martin Luther King Jr.?
questions.evidence: Unlike King, Mandela eventually decided that violence was necessary to bring about change. "We first broke the law in a way which avoided any recourse to violence; when this form was legislated against... only then did we decide to answer with violence," Mandela said.
choices: Mandela fought for equal rights for black citizens.
Mandela decided that violence was needed to bring about change.
Mandela organized protests and boycotts.
All of the above
questions.explanation: While both men fought for equal rights and organized protests, a KEY difference was that Mandela eventually decided violence was necessary to bring about change, while King believed in nonviolent resistance. Both organized protests and boycotts, so A and C were things they had in COMMON.

questions.question: Read this sentence: "Nelson Mandela wanted all races to be treated with dignity." What is another word for dignity?
questions.evidence: Mandela demanded rights for the prisoners and that all races be treated with dignity.
choices: kindness
fear
respect
happiness
questions.explanation: Dignity means worthiness of respect or esteem. When Mandela wanted all races to be treated with dignity, he wanted them to be treated with RESPECT.

questions.question: What did Mandela learn from Chief Jongintaba that helped him be successful?
questions.evidence: They went to meetings and listened as Chief Jongintaba settled arguments among his people. Nelson saw how the chief listened to both sides before making a decision. Nelson heard stories about those who came before him and learned about the ways of his people. He came to believe that he could be a leader.
choices: how to work hard and herd animals
strength and stamina
competitiveness and power
how to listen and be a leader
questions.explanation: From Chief Jongintaba, Mandela learned how to LISTEN to both sides before making a decision and how to be a LEADER. He used these listening skills throughout his life—even in prison, where he used them to unite prisoners of different races.

questions.question: What was the author's purpose for writing this book?
questions.evidence: The book is an informational biography that tells the true story of Nelson Mandela's life, from his childhood in a small village to his election as the first black president of South Africa.
choices: to explain the entire history of South Africa
to persuade readers to visit Nelson Mandela's homeland
to inform readers about the life and work of Nelson Mandela
to describe only the system of apartheid
questions.explanation: The author's purpose was to INFORM readers about the LIFE AND WORK of Nelson Mandela. While the book explains apartheid and South African history, those are part of telling Mandela's story—not the main purpose.

questions.question: Do you think Nelson Mandela's influence on others was always a positive influence? Use evidence from the text to support your answer.
questions.explanation: Think about how Mandela influenced people throughout his life. Consider both his peaceful leadership and his decision to support violence through the MK. Was every action positive, or were there complicated consequences?
opinionPrompts: What specific actions did Mandela take to influence others throughout his life?
Was Mandela's decision to form the Spear of the Nation (MK) a positive influence? What evidence supports your opinion?
How did Mandela's belief in reconciliation after 27 years in prison influence South Africa? What might have happened without it?

characterAnalysis.quote: "During my lifetime I have dedicated myself to this struggle of the African people. I have cherished the ideal of a democratic and free society in which all persons live together in harmony and with equal opportunities. It is an ideal which I hope to live for and to achieve. But if needs be, it is an ideal for which I am prepared to die." —from the text
characterAnalysis.quotePrompt: How does this quote reflect Mandela's personality? How did his personality lead to his accomplishments? How did his accomplishments influence the world?

Personality.prompt: What qualities made Nelson Mandela unique?
items: A natural leader from a young age — he came to believe he could be a leader while living with Chief Jongintaba
Deeply committed to justice — he would rather die than give up fighting for equality
Resilient and determined — spent 27 years in prison but never gave up his fight for freedom
A good listener — learned from Chief Jongintaba to listen to both sides before making decisions; used this skill even among prisoners
Believed in reconciliation, not revenge — chose to bring people together after apartheid rather than seeking revenge
Helped found the ANC Youth League (ANCYL) in 1944 to plan bigger protests and boycotts for equal rights
Led the Defiance Campaign in 1952, urging people to peacefully disobey apartheid laws
Survived a four-and-a-half-year treason trial (found not guilty) and the Rivonia Trial (sentenced to life)
Became the world's most famous political prisoner and symbol of the anti-apartheid movement during 27 years in prison
Elected the first black president of South Africa in 1994 in the country's first free elections
Started the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC) and shared the Nobel Peace Prize in 1993

Influence.prompt: How did Nelson Mandela affect the world?
items: Helped bring an end to apartheid, the official policy of racial segregation in South Africa
Established a country where all people are treated equally under the law, regardless of race
Started the Truth and Reconciliation Commission to help heal the country after decades of injustice
Shared the Nobel Peace Prize in 1993 with F. W. de Klerk for making great strides toward peace
Created the Nelson Mandela Children's Fund to help children in South Africa and around the world
Remains a lasting symbol of freedom for all people around the world

summarizeActivities.title: Who Was Nelson Mandela?
summarizeActivities.prompt: Write a summary of who Nelson Mandela was and why he is important. Include his birth, his fight against apartheid, his years in prison, and his election as president.

summarizeActivities.title: From Prison to President
summarizeActivities.prompt: Summarize the key events from Mandela's 27 years in prison to his election as president. What happened in order?

summarizeActivities.title: Mandela's Key Accomplishments
summarizeActivities.prompt: List and describe at least three of Nelson Mandela's most important accomplishments. Explain why each one matters.

summarizeActivities.title: Mandela's Legacy
summarizeActivities.prompt: Write a summary about what happened after Mandela retired from the presidency. What did he continue to do for his people and the world?

grammar.title: Commas in a Series
grammar.explanation: When we list three or more things, people, places, or actions in a sentence, we use commas to separate each item. This is called a 'series' or 'list.' The comma goes after each item except the last one. For example: 'Prisoners worked all day breaking rocks, ate bad food, and slept in tiny cells.' Notice the comma after 'rocks' and after 'food' — these separate the three things the prisoners did.
rules: Use commas to separate three or more items in a series (list)
Place a comma after each item EXCEPT the last one
The comma before 'and' or 'or' (the last comma) is called the 'serial comma' or 'Oxford comma' — it is recommended in most style guides
The items in a series can be nouns, verbs, adjectives, or phrases
Example from the book: 'They ate bad food, slept in tiny cells, and were treated poorly by the guards.' — three verb phrases separated by commas

fillInBlanks.sentence: Prisoners broke rocks, ate bad food, _____ slept in tiny cells.
fillInBlanks.hint: What word connects the last item in a series to the rest?

fillInBlanks.sentence: Nelson was happy playing with other boys, _____ sheep and cattle, and going to school.
fillInBlanks.hint: What did Nelson do with the sheep and cattle?

fillInBlanks.sentence: The friend brought Nelson into the ANC, a group that fought for equal rights, better jobs, _____ freedom.
fillInBlanks.hint: What word introduces the final item in a list?

fillInBlanks.sentence: Mandela demanded that prisoners have better food, _____, and more freedom to talk.
fillInBlanks.hint: What did the prisoners need so they could study?

fillInBlanks.sentence: He met with world leaders, advanced peace, _____ started a children's fund.
fillInBlanks.hint: What word connects the last item in a series?

fillInBlanks.sentence: The Dutch and British had colonized the land, forced people under control, _____ taken the riches.
fillInBlanks.hint: Three actions need two commas. What word goes before the last item?

exercises.sentence: They ate bad food, slept in tiny cells, and were treated poorly by the guards.
items: ate bad food
slept in tiny cells
were treated poorly by the guards

exercises.sentence: Nelson was happy playing with other boys, herding sheep and cattle, and going to school.
items: playing with other boys
herding sheep and cattle
going to school

exercises.sentence: He met with world leaders to advance his country and peace around the world, and he started the Nelson Mandela Children's Fund to help children.
exercises.note: This sentence does NOT contain a series of three or more items. The comma separates two independent clauses, not items in a list.

exercises.sentence: Mandela's early years in prison were hard, difficult, and _____.
exercises.explanation: In a series, each item should be the same part of speech. 'Hard' and 'difficult' are adjectives, so the third item must also be an adjective: 'painful.'

exercises.sentence: Prisoners broke rocks, ate bad food, _____ slept in tiny cells.
exercises.explanation: 'And' is used to connect the last item in a series. 'But' shows contrast and 'so' shows cause and effect — neither fits a simple list.

exercises.sentence: Nelson made friends moved into a tiny room and finished his college degree by mail.
exercises.corrected: Nelson made friends, moved into a tiny room, and finished his college degree by mail.
items: made friends
moved into a tiny room
finished his college degree by mail
exercises.explanation: Three actions in a series need commas after the first and second items: 'made friends, moved into a tiny room, and finished his college degree by mail.'

exercises.sentence: Blacks burned their travel passes sat down in Whites Only parts of buses and drank from Whites Only water fountains.
exercises.corrected: Blacks burned their travel passes, sat down in Whites Only parts of buses, and drank from Whites Only water fountains.
items: burned their travel passes
sat down in Whites Only parts of buses
drank from Whites Only water fountains
exercises.explanation: Three actions in a series need commas: 'burned their travel passes, sat down in Whites Only parts of buses, and drank from Whites Only water fountains.'

exercises.sentence: Mandela was a leader a symbol and a hero.
exercises.corrected: Mandela was a leader, a symbol, and a hero.
exercises.explanation: Three nouns in a series (leader, symbol, hero) need commas to separate them: 'Mandela was a leader, a symbol, and a hero.'

wordWork.title: Multiple-Meaning Words
wordWork.explanation: Many words in English have more than one meaning. The correct meaning depends on how the word is used in a sentence — this is called 'context.' For example, the word 'bar' can mean a place that serves drinks, a long piece of metal, or to block something. In 'Mandela was barred from giving speeches,' 'barred' means BLOCKED or FORBIDDEN — not a place or a piece of metal.
rules: A single word can have two or more meanings depending on context
Use the words AROUND the word (context clues) to figure out which meaning is intended
Common patterns: many verbs can be physical actions OR abstract actions (e.g., 'break' = shatter OR violate a law)
Many nouns can be concrete objects OR abstract ideas (e.g., 'charge' = electrical power OR an accusation)
Examples from the book: 'charged' (accused with a crime vs. filled with emotion); 'banned' (forbidden vs. beaten); 'founded' (established vs. based upon)

meanings: long pieces of metal or wood

sentences.sentence: The windows in Mandela's cell had iron bars.
The man sat at a bar and ordered a drink.
sentences.clue: ordered a drink → a place that serves drinks

meanings: filled with strong emotion or energy

sentences.sentence: Mandela was charged with conspiracy to bring down the government.
The room was charged with excitement as the crowd waited for the speaker.
sentences.clue: excitement + crowd waited → filled with emotion

sentences.sentence: In 1944, Nelson Mandela helped found the ANC Youth League.
sentences.clue: ANC Youth League → created an organization

sentences.sentence: His argument was well founded in years of research.
The store charged five dollars for the book.
sentences.clue: store + dollars → asked for a price

sentences.sentence: Mandela was charged with treason after the protest.
Nelson ran to catch the bus before it left.
Mandela ran South Africa as its president from 1994 to 1999.
Mandela read many books while he was in prison.
sentences.clue: read + prison → written works to read

sentences.sentence: The family booked their hotel room months ahead of the trip.
sentences.clue: hotel room + ahead of trip → reserved in advance

sentences.sentence: Nelson Mandela walked free for the first time in twenty-seven years.
sentences.clue: walked + twenty-seven years → no longer imprisoned

sentences.sentence: The school offered free lunch to every student.

evidence.question: What does the book tell us about the conditions on Robben Island where Mandela was imprisoned?
evidence.evidence: Prisoners worked all day breaking rocks. They ate bad food, slept in tiny cells, and were treated poorly by the guards. With no newspapers or radios, the prisoners were cut off from the rest of the world.
evidence.answer: Robben Island was a harsh prison. Prisoners broke rocks all day, ate bad food, slept in tiny cells, were treated poorly by guards, and were completely cut off from the outside world with no newspapers or radios.

evidence.question: What important lesson did Mandela learn from Chief Jongintaba?
evidence.evidence: Nelson saw how the chief listened to both sides before making a decision. Nelson heard stories about those who came before him and learned about the ways of his people. He came to believe that he could be a leader.
evidence.answer: Mandela learned how to LISTEN to both sides before making decisions. Chief Jongintaba showed him what it meant to be a fair leader. This skill helped Mandela throughout his life, even among prisoners on Robben Island.

evidence.question: How did the apartheid system change Mandela's approach to fighting for equal rights?
evidence.evidence: Mandela felt that all those years of peaceful protests had failed, and the ANC needed to try something else... Mandela slipped back into South Africa and started a new part of the ANC called the Spear of the Nation (MK). The MK wanted to force the people in power to listen to them. They planned to do this by using sabotage, such as bombing buildings or power stations.
evidence.answer: At first, Mandela used peaceful protests and boycotts. But when apartheid laws got worse and police killed 69 peaceful protesters, Mandela decided peaceful protests had failed. He formed the MK (Spear of the Nation) and used sabotage (bombings) to try to force change. His approach became more radical as conditions got worse.

evidence.question: What did Mandela's years in prison show about his character?
evidence.evidence: Mandela demanded rights for the prisoners and that all races be treated with dignity. The guards came to respect him... Mandela became a leader among the prisoners, who were men of different races. He used the listening skills he had learned as a child so that everyone felt he understood their side of the story.
evidence.answer: Even in prison for 27 years, Mandela kept fighting for justice and dignity for ALL people, not just himself. He used the listening skills he learned as a child to unite prisoners of different races. He refused to be freed unless the government ended apartheid, showing his commitment was stronger than his desire for personal freedom.

evidence.question: Why did Mandela create the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC)?
evidence.evidence: Mandela did not want his country filled with hate and fear over things that had happened in the past, so he helped start the Truth and Reconciliation Commission (TRC). A person could come before the TRC and confess to things he or she had done during apartheid and not be charged with a crime.
evidence.answer: Mandela created the TRC because he did NOT want South Africa to be filled with hate and fear about the past. The TRC allowed people to confess crimes from apartheid without being charged, so the truth could be told and the country could heal and move forward TOGETHER — through RECONCILIATION rather than punishment.

typing: Nelson Mandela was born in 1918 in a country that was very different from the South Africa of today.
Mandela, forty-four years old, had been sentenced to life in prison on Robben Island, a small island off the coast of South Africa.
Thousands of people stood in lines for up to five hours to vote for the first time. Nelson Mandela was elected president of South Africa.
Mandela's belief in talking with everyone, rather than just one side, helped to establish a country where all people are treated equally under the law.
Today, Nelson Mandela remains a lasting symbol of freedom for all.

robbenIsland.title: Life on Robben Island
robbenIsland.content: Nelson Mandela was sent to ROBben ISLAND at age 44 — SENTENCED to life in prison! Prisoners broke rocks all day, ate terrible food, slept in tiny cells, and were treated badly. Mandela was allowed only ONE short letter every 6 months — and it was CENSORED (parts removed by the prison)! He was allowed ONE visitor every 6 months through a glass window. How did he end up here?

robbenIsland.title: Sentenced & Censored
robbenIsland.content: SENTENCED = formally given a punishment by a court. Mandela was sentenced to LIFE in prison! CENSORED = had content removed. His letters were CENSORED by prison guards who didn't want him to receive certain information!

aFreeRoamingLife.title: Mandela's Early Life
aFreeRoamingLife.content: Mandela was born in 1918. The Dutch and British had COLONIZED South Africa — a small MINORITY of white people controlled all the good jobs and 90% of the land! His father was Chief Henry from a royal family, but a British official took his title and money. At age 7, his teacher gave him the British name 'Nelson.' At age 9, his father died and his life changed forever!

aFreeRoamingLife.title: Colonized & Minority
aFreeRoamingLife.content: COLONIZED = taken over by another country. The Dutch and British COLONIZED South Africa! MINORITY = a smaller group. A small MINORITY of white people had all the power and 90% of the land, even though black people made up 2/3 of the population!

learningToListen.title: The Great Place
learningToListen.content: Nelson went to live with Chief Jongintaba at the 'Great Place.' There he watched the chief LISTEN to both sides of arguments before making decisions. This is where Mandela LEARNED TO LISTEN — a skill he used his ENTIRE LIFE, even in prison! He came to believe he could be a LEADER.

theBigCity.title: From Village to Big City
theBigCity.content: At Fort Hare college, Mandela got in trouble for PROTESTING poor food — he quit the student council! He was kicked out. He and Justice ran away to Johannesburg instead of marrying the chief's chosen brides! In Johannesburg, Mandela finished his degree, studied law, and joined the ANC (African National Congress) — a group that fought for EQUAL RIGHTS for blacks.

theBigCity.title: Protesting & Boycotted
theBigCity.content: PROTESTING = demonstrating disagreement. Mandela PROTESTED poor food at college! BOYCOTTED = refused to buy or use something. People BOYCOTTED buses — they refused to ride them. After only 9 days, the fares were REDUCED! A boycott WORKED!

fromBadToWorse.title: The Horror of Apartheid
fromBadToWorse.content: In 1948, the National Party elected D.F. Malan as president — and he believed in APARTHEID (forced SEGREGATION). Black men had to carry PASSES to travel. Blacks couldn't be trained for good jobs. Families were SEPARATED because members were labeled different RACES. People couldn't marry across races, live together, or even meet in parks — those were SEGREGATED too!

fromBadToWorse.title: Apartheid & Segregated
fromBadToWorse.content: APARTHEID = racial segregation policy in South Africa. The word means 'apart-ness'! SEGREGATED = kept apart based on race. Parks, buses, water fountains, even schools were SEGREGATED — whites ONLY vs. everyone else. It was a terrible system of inequality!

aRisingStar.title: Mandela's Rising Activism
aRisingStar.content: Mandela led people to DEFY apartheid laws — sitting in 'Whites Only' areas! He was arrested and FORBIDDEN to travel, speak, or hold office. Charged with TREASON for 4 1/2 YEARS (found NOT GUILTY). In 1960, police KILLED 69 peaceful protesters! The ANC was BANNED. Mandela went into HIDING and formed the MK (Spear of the Nation) — using SABOTAGE (bombings). He became the 'Black Pimpernel' — slipping past police in DISGUISE! Charged with CONSPIRACY — guilty! The judge sentenced him to LIFE in prison instead of death.

aRisingStar.title: Sabotage & Conspiracy
aRisingStar.content: SABOTAGE = intentional destruction to make something not work. The MK used SABOTAGE — bombing buildings and power stations! CONSPIRACY = a secret plot to do something illegal. Mandela was charged with CONSPIRACY to bring down the government — a crime that could lead to the DEATH PENALTY! But the judge gave him LIFE in prison instead.

aRisingStar.title: Mandela's Final Words Before Prison
aRisingStar.content: Mandela thought he might be HANGED (executed). He prepared his FINAL WORDS: 'I have dedicated myself to this struggle of the African people... I have cherished the ideal of a democratic and free society... It is an ideal for which I am PREPARED TO DIE.' But the judge sentenced him to LIFE instead of death.

prisonYears.title: 27 Years in Prison
prisonYears.content: Mandela was in prison for 27 YEARS! Prisoners couldn't talk — but they left SECRET NOTES under rocks and toilet seats! Mandela demanded DIGNITY for ALL races — even the guards came to respect him! He became a LEADER among prisoners of ALL races. Outside prison, his family led protests and boycotts to keep his name ALIVE. Other countries BOYCOTTED South Africa (wouldn't trade). In 1989, President de Klerk finally let Mandela GO! In 1993, Mandela shared the NOBEL PEACE PRIZE with de Klerk!

prisonYears.title: Dignity
prisonYears.content: DIGNITY = self-respect and worthiness of respect. Even in prison, Mandela demanded that ALL races be treated with DIGNITY. He believed every person deserved respect — no matter their race. This belief helped change South Africa!

fiveHourLines.title: Freedom at Last!
fiveHourLines.content: In 1994 — South Africa's first FREE elections! People waited in lines for up to FIVE HOURS to vote! Mandela was elected PRESIDENT! He started the Truth and RECONCILIATION Commission (TRC) — so people could confess crimes from apartheid without being CHARGED. Mandela wanted the country to HEAL, not be filled with HATE! He retired after one term, moved back to his childhood village, started a Children's Fund, and kept working for PEACE until he died in 2013. 'Nelson Mandela remains a lasting symbol of FREEDOM FOR ALL.'

fiveHourLines.title: Reconciliation
fiveHourLines.content: RECONCILIATION = settling disagreements and becoming friendly again. The Truth and RECONCILIATION Commission (TRC) helped South Africa HEAL after apartheid. Instead of punishing everyone, Mandela chose RECONCILIATION — bringing people TOGETHER so the country could move forward in PEACE!
